title: Pamela Z

infobox.value: (born 1956) is an American [composer](/wiki/Composer "Composer"), performer, and [media artist](/wiki/New_media_art "New media art") best known for her solo works for voice with electronic processing. In performance, she combines various vocal sounds including operatic [bel canto](/wiki/Bel_canto "Bel canto"), experimental [extended techniques](/wiki/Vocal_extended_technique "Vocal extended technique") and [spoken word](/wiki/Spoken_word "Spoken word"), with samples and sounds generated by manipulating found objects.[[1]](#cite_note-EastBay-1) Z's musical aesthetic is one of sonic accretion, and she typically processes her voice in real time through the software program [Max](/wiki/MaxMSP "MaxMSP") on a [MacBook Pro](/wiki/MacBook_Pro "MacBook Pro") as a means of layering, looping, and altering her live vocal sound.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Her performance work often includes video projections and special controllers with sensors that allow her to use physical gestures to manipulate the sound and projected media.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American singer

| Pamela Z | |
| --- | --- |

| Background information | |
| Born | 1956 (age 69–70)  [Buffalo, New York](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") |
| Genres | Avant-garde, contemporary classical, experimental, electroacoustic |
| Occupations | Composer, performer |
| Instruments | Voice, electronics |
| Labels | [Starkland](/wiki/Starkland "Starkland"), [Innova](/wiki/Innova_Recordings "Innova Recordings"), [Bridge](/wiki/Bridge_Records "Bridge Records") |

Musical artist

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Pamela_Z_at_the_University_of_Colorado.jpg/500px-Pamela_Z_at_the_University_of_Colorado.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pamela_Z_at_the_University_of_Colorado.jpg)

Pamela Z performing at the [University of Colorado](/wiki/University_of_Colorado_Boulder "University of Colorado Boulder")'s ATLAS Institute, [Boulder](/wiki/Boulder,_Colorado "Boulder, Colorado") in 2012

**Pamela Z** (born 1956) is an American [composer](/wiki/Composer "Composer"), performer, and [media artist](/wiki/New_media_art "New media art") best known for her solo works for voice with electronic processing. In performance, she combines various vocal sounds including operatic [bel canto](/wiki/Bel_canto "Bel canto"), experimental [extended techniques](/wiki/Vocal_extended_technique "Vocal extended technique") and [spoken word](/wiki/Spoken_word "Spoken word"), with samples and sounds generated by manipulating found objects.[[1]](#cite_note-EastBay-1) Z's musical aesthetic is one of sonic accretion, and she typically processes her voice in real time through the software program [Max](/wiki/MaxMSP "MaxMSP") on a [MacBook Pro](/wiki/MacBook_Pro "MacBook Pro") as a means of layering, looping, and altering her live vocal sound.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Her performance work often includes video projections and special controllers with sensors that allow her to use physical gestures to manipulate the sound and projected media.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Z's solo albums are *Echolocation* (1987), and *[A Delay is Better](/wiki/A_Delay_is_Better "A Delay is Better")* (2004), and *A Secret Code* (2021). Her fixed-media sound works for radio and [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media") [installations](/wiki/Installation_art "Installation art") for art galleries have manifested in solo exhibitions at the [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art") (MoMA, New York), the [Krannert Art Museum](/wiki/Krannert_Art_Museum "Krannert Art Museum") ([Champaign, Illinois](/wiki/Champaign,_Illinois "Champaign, Illinois")), Savvy Contemporary (Berlin), Trondheim Elektroniske Kunstsenter ([Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim "Trondheim"), Norway), the Fine Arts Center Galleries at [Bowling Green State University](/wiki/Bowling_Green_State_University "Bowling Green State University"), and the Chico University Art Gallery ([Chico, California](/wiki/Chico,_California "Chico, California")), and in group exhibitions in the [Dakar Biennale](/wiki/Dakar_Biennale "Dakar Biennale"), Sénégal, the [Whitney Museum of American Art](/wiki/Whitney_Museum_of_American_Art "Whitney Museum of American Art"), [San Francisco Arts Commission](/wiki/San_Francisco_Arts_Commission "San Francisco Arts Commission") Gallery, Erzbischöfliches Diözesanmuseum, [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), and the [McColl Center for Visual Art](/wiki/McColl_Center_for_Visual_Art "McColl Center for Visual Art"), [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina").

In addition to being a Robert Rauschenberg Foundation Artist in Residence, Z has received a [United States Artists](/wiki/United_States_Artists "United States Artists") fellowship, the [American Academy of Arts and Letters](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Letters "American Academy of Arts and Letters") Walter Hinrichsen Award, the [Rome Prize](/wiki/Rome_Prize "Rome Prize"), a [Guggenheim Fellowship](/wiki/Guggenheim_Fellowship "Guggenheim Fellowship"), the [MIT](/wiki/MIT "MIT") McDermott Award in the Arts, the [Doris Duke Performing Artist Award](/wiki/Doris_Duke_Performing_Artist_Award "Doris Duke Performing Artist Award") in theater, the CalArts [Alpert Award in the Arts](/wiki/Alpert_Awards_in_the_Arts "Alpert Awards in the Arts"), the [Creative Capital](/wiki/Creative_Capital "Creative Capital") Fund, the [ASCAP](/wiki/ASCAP "ASCAP") Music Award (for 17 years), the MAP Fund (twice), and an [NEA](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts"), and Japan/US Friendship Commission Fellowship. Her work and performances have been reviewed in the [*New York Times*](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times"), the *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")*, *[East Bay Express](/wiki/East_Bay_Express "East Bay Express")*, [the *Wire*](/wiki/The_Wire_(magazine) "The Wire (magazine)"), and [the *Washington Post*](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post"), among other places.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Born in [Buffalo, New York](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), and raised in the [Denver Metro area](/wiki/Denver_metropolitan_area "Denver metropolitan area"), Pamela Z received her bachelor's degree in music from the [University of Colorado at Boulder](/wiki/University_of_Colorado_at_Boulder "University of Colorado at Boulder")[[1]](#cite_note-EastBay-1) in 1978, where she studied [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") voice. In the late 1970s and early 1980s, she worked as a [singer-songwriter](/wiki/Singer-songwriter "Singer-songwriter") on voice and guitar throughout Colorado under the name **Pam Brooks.**

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

After beginning to experiment with digital delay and reverb for live vocal processing in the 1980s and composing new works involving live looping,[[4]](#cite_note-4) she relocated to [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") in 1984, where she legally changed her last name to Z and became active in the San Francisco Bay Area [contemporary music](/wiki/Contemporary_classical_music "Contemporary classical music") and [performance art](/wiki/Performance_art "Performance art") scene. Throughout the late 1980s and the '90s, she continued to create solo voice and electronics performances, and gained visibility through her appearances in area new music performance venues, theaters, and art galleries. She began touring her work nationally and internationally and, by 2000, she was performing regularly in New York City, Europe, and Japan.
Z has performed in such festivals as [Bang on a Can](/wiki/Bang_on_a_Can "Bang on a Can") at [Lincoln Center](/wiki/Lincoln_Center "Lincoln Center") in New York City, the Interlink Festival in Japan, [Other Minds](/wiki/Other_Minds_(organization) "Other Minds (organization)") in San Francisco, [La Biennale di Venezia](/wiki/La_Biennale_di_Venezia "La Biennale di Venezia") in Venice, and [Pina Bausch Tanztheater](/wiki/Pina_Bausch "Pina Bausch")'s Festival in [Wuppertal](/wiki/Wuppertal "Wuppertal"), Germany.

In addition to her solo voice and electronics works, Z has composed [chamber works](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music") commissioned by contemporary music soloists and ensembles such as [Kronos Quartet](/wiki/Kronos_Quartet "Kronos Quartet"), the [Bang on a Can All Stars](/wiki/Bang_on_a_Can_All_Stars "Bang on a Can All Stars"), [Eighth Blackbird](/wiki/Eighth_Blackbird "Eighth Blackbird"),[[1]](#cite_note-EastBay-1) flautist [Claire Chase](/wiki/Claire_Chase "Claire Chase"), the New York string quartet [ETHEL](/wiki/Ethel_(string_quartet) "Ethel (string quartet)"), [Del Sol Quartet](/wiki/Del_Sol_Quartet "Del Sol Quartet"), the [California EAR Unit](/wiki/California_EAR_Unit "California EAR Unit"), the [San Francisco Contemporary Music Players](/wiki/San_Francisco_Contemporary_Music_Players "San Francisco Contemporary Music Players"), the Left Coast Chamber Ensemble, and [Orchestra of St. Luke's](/wiki/Orchestra_of_St._Luke%27s "Orchestra of St. Luke's").

In 2013 she was commissioned by the [Kronos Quartet](/wiki/Kronos_Quartet "Kronos Quartet") to produce a new work, "And the Movement of the Tongue," at the [Yerba Buena Center for the Arts](/wiki/Yerba_Buena_Center_for_the_Arts "Yerba Buena Center for the Arts"). *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")'*s music critic Joshua Kosman described the work as "witty and beautifully touching" and "encapsulat[ing] the vivacity" of San Francisco.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The next year she created the soundscape for Jo Kreiter's “Multiple Mary and Invisible Jane,” a free 30-minute show that took place on an 80-foot wall of [University of California College of the Law, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California_College_of_the_Law,_San_Francisco "University of California College of the Law, San Francisco"). Z sampled and processed voices of homeless women telling their personal stories.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Z has also composed scores for modern choreographers [Stephan Koplowitz](/wiki/Stephan_Koplowitz "Stephan Koplowitz"), [Brenda Way](/wiki/Brenda_Way "Brenda Way") ([ODC Dance](/wiki/ODC_Dance "ODC Dance")), and Mary Armentrout.

In 2022 Z was one of several composers commissioned by soprano [Julia Bullock](/wiki/Julia_Bullock "Julia Bullock") to create new work for her *History's Persistent Voice* for the [San Francisco Symphony](/wiki/San_Francisco_Symphony "San Francisco Symphony").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In addition, she has composed and recorded [film scores](/wiki/Film_score "Film score") for independent filmmakers including [Barbara Hammer](/wiki/Barbara_Hammer "Barbara Hammer"), [Lynne Sachs](/wiki/Lynne_Sachs "Lynne Sachs"), [Jeanne C. Finley](/wiki/Jeanne_C._Finley "Jeanne C. Finley") and John Muse.

## Recordings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Recordings")]

Studio recordings of several of Pamela Z's signature pieces appear on her 2004 solo CD, *[A Delay is Better](/wiki/A_Delay_is_Better "A Delay is Better")* on the [Starkland](/wiki/Starkland "Starkland") label. In addition, a number of her works have been released on various [experimental music](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music") and sound art compilations including her "Declaratives In First Person" on *[Crosstalk: American Speech Music](/wiki/Crosstalk:_American_Speech_Music "Crosstalk: American Speech Music")* a 2008 Bridge Records compilation produced by [Mendi + Keith Obadike](/wiki/Obadike "Obadike"), and ‘’Geekspeak’’, which appears both on *Sonic Circuits IV*, a 1996 [Innova Recordings](/wiki/Innova_Recordings "Innova Recordings") compilation and on *Bitstreams*, a [Whitney Museum](/wiki/Whitney_Museum "Whitney Museum") collection of works from a 2001 sound exhibition curated by [Stephen Vitiello](/wiki/Stephen_Vitiello "Stephen Vitiello"). Z also recorded a track for [Meredith Monk](/wiki/Meredith_Monk "Meredith Monk")’s 2012 tribute CD *Monk Mix*– performing a voice and electronics arrangement of Monk’s ‘’Scared Song’’.

In 2021, her reissue of ‘Echolocation’ was included in the [*New York Times*](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")'s list of "5 Classical Music Albums to Hear Right Now." Seth Colter Walls wrote, "Given her skill at live looping and solo concertizing, it’s a treat to hear her in bandleader mode.... Bridging ... diverse reference points, as ever, is Z’s own virtuosic vocal technique, which incorporates both her bel canto training as well as her eclectic listening, across genres."[[8]](#cite_note-NYT2021-8)

## Visual art work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Visual art work")]

Pamela Z has created fixed-media sound works for radio and [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media") [installations](/wiki/Installation_art "Installation art") for art galleries. Her 21-channel sound installation, *Simultaneous*, and performances of the accompanying multimedia chamber work were presented at the [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art") (MoMA, New York) as part of her 2023 Studio Residency.[[9]](#cite_note-9) She had a solo exhibition at the [Krannert Art Museum](/wiki/Krannert_Art_Museum "Krannert Art Museum") in [Champaign, Illinois](/wiki/Champaign,_Illinois "Champaign, Illinois") in 2010,[[10]](#cite_note-10) the performance component of which she played at Theater Artaud in San Francisco the same year.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Other exhibition spaces include Savvy Contemporary (Berlin), the annual New Music Festival at the Fine Arts Center Galleries at [Bowling Green State University](/wiki/Bowling_Green_State_University "Bowling Green State University") in 2013,[[12]](#cite_note-12) and the Chico University Art Gallery ([Chico, California](/wiki/Chico,_California "Chico, California")).

Z's installations have been shown in group exhibitions including *Walkmen* at the Erzbischöfliches Diözesanmuseum in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), Germany[[1]](#cite_note-EastBay-1) in 2000 for which she composed "…and on your left…" to be experienced on headphones,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and she presented "Sonic Gestures," a six-channel video installation at TEKS Trondheim Elektroniske Kunstsenter in [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim "Trondheim"), Norway in 2019.[[14]](#cite_note-14) She was included in *Bitstreams* at the [Whitney Museum of American Art](/wiki/Whitney_Museum_of_American_Art "Whitney Museum of American Art") in 2001,[[15]](#cite_note-15) *Dak’Art* ([Dakar Biennale](/wiki/Dakar_Biennale "Dakar Biennale"), Sénégal), *side by side/in the world,* a 2019 exhibition of California artists addressing the concept of sanctuary at the [San Francisco Arts Commission](/wiki/San_Francisco_Arts_Commission "San Francisco Arts Commission") Gallery,[[16]](#cite_note-16) and she was an artist-in-residence at the [McColl Center for Visual Art](/wiki/McColl_Center_for_Visual_Art "McColl Center for Visual Art"), [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina") in 2002 and 2012.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Narration work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Narration work")]

Z is also known for her narration work in independent film and television. Her voice appears in several documentaries including [Sam Green](/wiki/Sam_Green "Sam Green")'s [*The Weather Underground*](/wiki/The_Weather_Underground_(film) "The Weather Underground (film)") (2002), Hrabba Gunnarsdottir's *Alive in Limbo*, and the Bay Area [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") affiliate [KQED](/wiki/KQED_(TV) "KQED (TV)")'s 2003-2016 weekly arts television program, *Spark*.

## Honors and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Honors and awards")]

Z has received the [United States Artists](/wiki/United_States_Artists "United States Artists") fellowship, the [American Academy of Arts and Letters](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Letters "American Academy of Arts and Letters") Walter Hinrichsen Award (both 2020), the [Rome Prize](/wiki/Rome_Prize "Rome Prize") (2019),[[1]](#cite_note-EastBay-1) the [Guggenheim Fellowship](/wiki/Guggenheim_Fellowship "Guggenheim Fellowship") (2004); [Doris Duke Performing Artist Award](/wiki/Doris_Duke_Performing_Artist_Award "Doris Duke Performing Artist Award") in theater (2015);[[1]](#cite_note-EastBay-1) the CalArts [Alpert Award in the Arts](/wiki/Alpert_Awards_in_the_Arts "Alpert Awards in the Arts") (1998); the [Creative Capital](/wiki/Creative_Capital "Creative Capital") Fund (2002); the [ASCAP](/wiki/ASCAP "ASCAP") Music Award (2000-2017); the MAP Fund (2009 and 2012); a [SEAMUS](/wiki/SEAMUS "SEAMUS") Lifetime Achievement Award (2016), and the [NEA](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts") and Japan/US Friendship Commission Fellowship (1998). In 2008 she was honored as Alumna of the Year by the [University of Colorado at Boulder](/wiki/University_of_Colorado_at_Boulder "University of Colorado at Boulder") College of Music, and she received a [Prix Ars Electronica](/wiki/Prix_Ars_Electronica "Prix Ars Electronica") ([Linz](/wiki/Linz "Linz"), Austria) [honorable mention](/wiki/Honorable_mention "Honorable mention") in the Digital Musics Category. In 2017, she was a Robert Rauschenberg Foundation Artist in Residence.

In 2018, the [New Interfaces for Musical Expression](/wiki/New_Interfaces_for_Musical_Expression "New Interfaces for Musical Expression") (NIME) conference established the “Pamela Z Award for Innovation”, an annual prize for recognition of “researchers who are positively contributing to bringing more diversity to the NIME community”.

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Discography")]

### Solo albums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Solo albums")]

* *Echolocation,* 1987, ZED, cassette only, out-of-print[[8]](#cite_note-NYT2021-8)
* *[A Delay is Better](/wiki/A_Delay_is_Better "A Delay is Better")*, 2004, [Starkland](/wiki/Starkland "Starkland"), CD ST-213
* *A Secret Code,* 2021, Neuma Records[[18]](#cite_note-18) CD 143
* *Echolocation*  (reissue, 2021), Freedom to Spend[[8]](#cite_note-NYT2021-8) Vinyl LP FTS-024
* *Simultaneous*, 2025, [Other Minds Records](/wiki/Other_Minds_Records "Other Minds Records"), LP OM3005

### Compilations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Compilations")]

* *Pearls, the Gem of the Sea* on " Komotion International Vol. 11", compilation, 1991, Spirit Records, [LP](/wiki/LP_record "LP record"), CD, CS
* *State* on "State of the Union", compilation produced by [Elliott Sharp](/wiki/Elliott_Sharp "Elliott Sharp"), 1992, Arrest, CD
* *In Tymes of Olde* (Z) and *Obsession, Additiction and the Aristotelian Curve* (Z and Imhoff) on "From A to Z" compilation, [Starkland](/wiki/Starkland "Starkland"), 1993, CD
* *Bald Boyfriend* performed by The Qube Chix on "Dice" compilation, 1993, Ishtar, CD
* *Geekspeak* on "Sonic Circuits IV" compilation, 1996, [Innova Recordings](/wiki/Innova_Recordings "Innova Recordings"), CD
* *Parts* and *Questions/Trip* on "Dice 2" compilation, 1996, Ishtar, CD
* *Caught* on "Emergency Music" compilation, CRI, 1998, CD
* *Live/Work* on "IMMERSION" compilation, Starkland, 2000, 5.1 surround DVD-audio ST-2010
* *Geekspeak* on "Bitstreams", compilation curated by [Stephen Vitiello](/wiki/Stephen_Vitiello "Stephen Vitiello"), Whitney Museum of American Art, 2001, CD
* *50 for Charles Amirkhanian* on "Homo Sonorus" compilation, Kunstradio, 2001, CD
* *No. 3* on "Visions", compilation, (2002) EMIT Series, CD
* *Pop Titles 'You'* on "Deep Wireless 2: New Adventures in Sound Art," 2005, CD
* *Declaratives In First Person* on "Crosstalk: American Speech Music" compilation produced by [Mendi & Keith Obadike](/wiki/Mendi_%26_Keith_Obadike "Mendi & Keith Obadike"), [Bridge Records](/wiki/Bridge_Records "Bridge Records"), 2008, CD

### Tribute CDs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Tribute CDs")]

* *Movements I, II, & III* ([Peter Kowald](/wiki/Peter_Kowald "Peter Kowald")), Kowald, Gottschalk, and Z on "Global Village Trio", Free Elephant, 2004, CD
* *Postcard From Heaven* ([John Cage](/wiki/John_Cage "John Cage")), Victoria Jordanova: harps, Z: voices, Arpaviva, 2006, CD
* *Scared Song* ([Meredith Monk](/wiki/Meredith_Monk "Meredith Monk")), composer, Monk, arr. & performer, Z on "Monk Mix", House Foundation for the Arts, 2012, CD

### On others' albums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pamela_Z&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: On others' albums")]

* Ethel, *Ethel Dreams of Temporal Disturbances* (Z) on "Light", Cantaloupe, 2006, CD CA20137
* [Vijay Iyer](/wiki/Vijay_Iyer "Vijay Iyer") and [Mike Ladd](/wiki/Mike_Ladd "Mike Ladd"), *[Still Life with Commentator](/wiki/Still_Life_with_Commentator "Still Life with Commentator"),* Savoy Jazz, 2007
* Lisle Ellis, *Sucker Punch Requiem* (Ellis: bass, Z: voice & electronics, [Oliver Lake](/wiki/Oliver_Lake "Oliver Lake"): saxophones, George Lewis: trombone, Holly Hoffman: flutes, [Mike Wofford](/wiki/Mike_Wofford "Mike Wofford"): piano, [Susie Ibarra](/wiki/Susie_Ibarra "Susie Ibarra"): drums & percussion), Henceforth, 2008, CD
* Vijay Iyer and Mike Ladd, *[Holding It Down: The Veterans' Dreams Project](/wiki/Holding_It_Down:_The_Veterans%27_Dreams_Project "Holding It Down: The Veterans' Dreams Project"),* 2013
